First of all, and you heard this, Leonardo da Vinci already said it. It's easier to resist at the beginning than at the end. And this is the foundation, of course, of the principle of consistency and commitment. And for me, this principle is the most powerful principle of all. And there's research. Why I think that because actually in the Western European countries, the first thing we ask ourselves when we want when we get a request is. Did I previously made a commitment to this? Statement to this request. But there's much more to this principle because when we make a choice, we encounter personal and interpersonal pressure. To behave consistently with what we previously have said or done. So the question is, and I like to ask that, why is this so powerful? Patrick, what? What what's your take on that? Why is that so powerful that it interpersonal? And I think we don't want to. I don't want to put you on the spot. This is just open learning. Yeah, I think because we don't want other people to think ill of us. If we if we say one thing and then do something else, then we feel they can less depend on us or we're less trustworthy. Definitely, there's a social pressure to behave consistently because what would happen if you not do it? People call you a liar, not trustworthy, things like that. Not good names. But there's one even more powerful element. Did you do my oh, I see Leopold. Yeah, I think that's an issue of integrity and self-reputation. So we don't want to see us breaking our integrity or. Yeah, also completely right. Although think about you as masters of ethical persuasion. You have to work hard. Why is it powerful that people feel an interpersonal pressure? I will give the answer. Oh, John, you. Oh, no. Yeah. Oh, yeah. Great. I'll give it a shot. I think it has a motivational thing for people internally. That means if you say you're going to do something and you've got a group of people here, it's a sort of open promise. And if you if you don't actually follow through, then people think, oh, God, he he's not, you know, he's not going to do anything. He's not, you know, he's not consistent and he's not willing to do what he says he's going to do. Also, I completely agree. The public commitment. Fantastic. The answer what I was looking for and sorry, I make your life hard. Everybody was and it's the most common mistake about when people tried to use or think they use this principle. They think I am being consistent. So I act consistent with what I promise with what I have said. And yes, that's a great idea, of course, because otherwise people don't trust you. But the principle is not about that. The principle is not about you being consistent with others. It's about the other person is being consistent with themself. And what happens if people make small little steps in your direction? They will do the hard work for you. That's why I love this principle. It's a principle that. Works over time. Because they will they will start persuading themself to say yes to you. So you don't have to do the hard work all the time. It's planting the seeds. So they move themself in your direction. And to introduce that, how do 90 to 95 percent of the conversations start in the world? That's a personal statistic, not scientific, but on my take, I think if I would do the research, this is quite well. The far majority of people opens a conversation and says, this is what I want. Let's say it's a 50 50 odds. So I lose 50 percent of my audience, not making commitment, actually making commitment against me. So that's not the most wise opening of getting to where I want. So how can we improve that? And let me introduce you this man, World Champion Debating. He is from Holland, Sharon Cruz. He's Dutch, European and World Champion in debating. And he's a speaker at the biggest persuasion event in the Netherlands, which I know, Albert, you will be dancing your hometown in Utrecht, maybe Diederik also from the Netherlands. And since then, he brought him every year. So he made the Chaldini name big in the Netherlands. Hans Janssen from Dank Productions, which means thinking productions. But what Sharon teach and he always teach how his application on consistency is, is finding the point of mutual acceptance. And I love his technique because so I honestly stole this technique from him. It's brilliant because it's simple. So what he says is you have a standpoint. You want to say, this is what I want to achieve. But how do I bring that to my target of influence? Well, let's start with five questions. The first question is why? Why? Somebody wants to have a four day work week. Then you have an answer and then ask again, why? I, by being trained by Sharon, I know there is very complicated jargon about this. It's called the double why. I see you raising your hand to the appart. You're aware of this. Yeah, go ahead. Then you ask, why is it important you find an answer? And then you start to think about it. And then you start kicking in some open doors. Then you ask again, why is it important? And you ask again, why it's important? And there's your point of mutual acceptance. After these questions, you find a statement that probably everybody in your environment will agree to. So let's do the exercise. I want a four day work week. Why? Well, then we have fewer burnout. Why? That leads to happier relations. Why is that important? Well, that's a better well being. Why is that important? Well, that's good for your health. Why is that important? You feel the open door kicking in the open doors. Well, then we live longer and that leads to more happiness. There's a point of mutual acceptance. Why? There's nobody on earth that would say, if I say, do you think we should live longer and have more happy happiness in our life? I don't believe there's anybody on earth. Maybe. OK, nobody is always that's a 99.99% would say yes. So do you agree we should live longer and have more happiness? Yes, commitment one. Well, I agree as well. And I think it's good for our health. Yes, I agree. Commitment two. And I think that leads to better well being and we create much happier relations. Well, I think so as well. And I think also that would lead to fewer burnouts, which nowadays is a huge, that would lead to fewer burnouts. Yeah, I think so as well. And therefore I'd say we need a four day work week. You feel the difference. It's a super simple exercise by building it up and finding the point of mutual acceptance. You build up your statement. You build up your commitment. If you do so. Everybody can get at least two to three commitments. Leopold, please reflect because you obviously know this technique. Yeah, thank you. I love it actually because I learned that in design thinking. And there's a very similarity in terms of process. What I realized and correct me if I'm wrong is the more specific you are in the answers, the more you can actually lead to the to the commitment. So if it's just good for your health, it's still very abstract, right? Even live longer. It's still very abstract, but it's live more than 80 years and wake up every day feeling blah, blah, blah. So specificity, I think builds more commitment in that regard. I would love to hear your point. So how can we get more commitments and that's one thing you heard the endowed pro sorry a commitment without a commitment so you probably are all aware about this study there was at a gas station they had loyalty cards and they say well if you get eight stamps you come to get gas eight times you get a free car wash then what they did there were 10 stamps two were already pre-stamped and again if you have the rest you get a free car wash. Fun thing is when they only had eight stamps it was 19% of the people completed it the loyalty program one nine then when they just changed the card with two pre-stamped it was 34% completed the loyalty program and they completed quicker they came quicker back they came more in a more quicker pace back to the gas station but going from 19 to 34% that's an uptake of 178% that's not bad for redesigning your loyalty card right so what this effect says the endowed progress effect is people feel when they see or made a commitment previously they want to move forward only it's so powerful that even if the people didn't make the commitment but you made it for them but it still works so the questionnaire is how can you do this it's not about loyalty programs only it's about showing for example in a sales process show the process of where you are and say we always do an intake then we do a presentation then we do the advice and if you show that timeline from one to max five steps and concluding question based on those arguments to achieve a desirable response well that's the one sentence that says it all about the principle of consistency and that man is of course socrates only there are some one fun fact that fascinates me socrates is born in 469 what the documents say i was not there before christ so it's about two and a half thousand years ago and he was a philosopher but what those words said in greeks philo is love so far as wisdom so a philosopher had to laugh for wisdom and then this was his wisdom the only true wisdom is knowing you know nothing so this was the most recognized philosopher the guy who loved wisdom was most recognized for his love of wisdom and he's telling us the only wisdom is there is knowing you know nothing why i love this it's one how many people perceive this sentence it's about you need to keep on learning which i agree to but now we go to conversations and now people stop remembering this sentence but it's i believe more true you don't know nothing about that person you interact but you don't open you have always your own goal so it's a wrong question from reciprocity point of view but you don't know what these people want but by art but you know what you want but by asking questions in a certain way you not only get to know it you move them into a direction so in a very easy way it's somebody has a believer statement or conclusion somebody wants to make somebody saying yes to it so you can look from that that point of view so let's say you want the four-day work week you can go and ask somebody else you want to persuade somebody so you have the original source what would it lead to the implications the opposing thoughts and the supporting thoughts and this sounds very vague and i can teach a full day about socratic question technique and i think i see leopold smiling he can do as well well only i want to give you the first take away and there's so much free learning online about this the thing what i want to show you is that is basically very simple and start simple because if you start complicated we'll stop using it but socrates it's called socratic question technique and in action there are six like you see here and nowadays there are also models that have nine or eight even but let's start with six there are six types of questions so first the clarification questions which could look what do you mean by what's your main point could you put this in another way you feel here becoming a sleuth of influence a master of ethical communication for me this is ultimate communication by mastering this then you go in probing assumptions and for the dutch who don't know probe it's dorfrager so it's keep on answering asking questions that's funny if you're dutch you know which english words we are not used to to use so what could we assume instead is it always the case but you feel the effect if you would do this in a conversation the other person is talking if the other person is talking and that was what i told you at the beginning of this mastermind the most common mistake of using the principle of commitment and consistency is that we think we have to be consistent is the other person so the more questions we ask the more commitments we get if they are not the commitments we want to have we should be able to ask different questions if we study the model of socratic question technique we can reframe these questions we have them in our toolbox and we can direct the conversation that's my take on it because what are you assuming is a complete different question than you seem to be assuming how would you justify you see it directs 180 degrees the conversation in a different way we are still at the same phase of probing assumptions my apologies that i slightly have to rush these stages but i definitely wanted to bring to your attention maybe this is fun in the Netherlands or online no let's do it online not everybody can come to the net so then you have probing rationale challenging the rational aspect why i like that is an at three is because we know system one takes control but i know if you dive really deep into the Nobel Prize winning research there is a fact there is a part of our brain that likes rational of course making decisions not at the beginning not at the end of finally making the decision that's what system one system two says but in the decision process which what we are talking now building up commitments yes so a set of great questions then questioning the viewpoints probing consequences and then questioning the find that fascinating when i talk to people because you might be talking about leadership uh but what you mean is completely different than what i mean so i always say to define is is divine so oh i proposals and winning proposal from 30 to 35 percent so i'm thinking now what should i highlight should i highlight that they're missing on the rest the you know 65 percent or that there's just five percent remaining to achieve your goal am i making sense great question yes it makes a lot of sense and in this case the scarcity approach is much better because if you can highlight what the five percent is missing out because they didn't make any commitment in that conversation how you now describe it from 30 to 35 it's not their commitment is their goal is their goal yes and that's the thing you good afternoon to everyone very very happy to and excited to have you all here i use this technique of what you told today in my handling objection it's a very simple one the first question is any customer raises an objection you ask this question what do you mean by that and they will answer and then if required you can further probe and then use this one small commitment saying that if i understand you correctly what you're saying is this is this right and he says right then the next one is apart from this what you mentioned is there anything else that is of concern to you and the customer says no you're lucky if he says yes you're still lucky because you can go back and ask what is the concern that you have okay or somebody will say you answer this first but if they say no and answer this first take the small commitment before you answer if i can give you a satisfying answer to this can we move forward now and the customer will say yes then you're lucky if he says no you go back to him and say what is that which is still preventing you to do that then you give the whatever the best possible answer and then say can we move forward that's another commitment that you give thank you for sharing right i hope this session was valuable again and thank you for joining and looking forward to seeing you all very soon thank you very much bye bye
